notes.note: Leucas, an island off western Greece; the section is a day-by-day log of the homeward voyage from Cilicia, naming each port in turn.
A stade is roughly 185 metres, so about 22 km; the short hop to Cassiope on Corcyra's north-east coast, the jumping-off point for the crossing to Italy.
Hydruntum (modern Otranto), the nearest Italian landfall on the heel of the peninsula when crossing from Corcyra.
Brundisium (modern Brindisi), the great port of south-east Italy and the standard arrival point for travellers from Greece and the East.
Gnaeus Plancius, the man whom Cicero had defended in the Pro Plancio (54 BC); his slave delivers Tiro's letter at Brundisium.
Asclapo, the physician at Patrae attending the sick Tiro.
Lyso of Patrae was Tiro's host; Cicero fears the strain of attending Lyson's musical entertainment ('symphonia') will cause a relapse.
Ancient medicine reckoned the danger points of a fever by weeks ('hebdomades'); the fourth week was thought a critical moment for a recurrence.
Manius Curius, the Roman banker resident at Patrae who had taken the ailing Tiro into his house; Cicero arranges through him to pay the doctor.
Lucius Mescinius Rufus, Cicero's quaestor in Cilicia, also making the homeward crossing; Cicero recommends his cautious seamanship as a safe companion for Tiro.
